title: 第 43 節
en: You remember the Fins, of whom I spoke to you in my hrst course of Lectures. They belong neither to the Aryan nor to the Semitic stock. They speak one of the Ural-Altaic group of languages. They now live partly in Sweden, partly in Russia. You may remember how Mr. Gladstone ^, when lately in Scotland, stood up for them as one of the oppressed nationalities of the world. Whether they are fit for Home-rule, and whether Mr. Gladstone's advocacy is likely to secure to them the benefits of Home-rule, and at the same time seats in the Imperial Parliament at St. Petersburg — whenever there is such a Parliament— are matters that do not concern us. But what concerns us is, that among the peasants of Finland, among people ignorant of reading and writing, large fragments of epic poetry have been discovered during the first half of our century, entirely preserved by oral tradition, never written before, either by the poet or by his admirers, and yet easily fitted together into one epic poem. I wish I had time to explain to you the process by which these poems had been preserved, and at last have been collected, printed, critically edited, and translated-. But I think you ' See Times, Oct. 29, 1890. '' See Appendix VIII.

SOUL AFTER DEATH. 313 will have seen, even from these short remarks, in what sense popular poetry, such as the Homeric poems, for instance, may be said to reflect not only the thoughts of one poetic mind, but at the same time the thoughts of many people, who would not have listened to. that is to say, who would not have allowed any poetry to survive, except what they themselves approved of.
The Nekyia does not represent the popular Belief.
The question now arises. May we take what Homer says or implies about the dead, may we, more particularly, take his Nekyia, as reflecting the general ideas of the Greeks at his time ? I doubt it.

First of all, what do people mean by his time ? That there may have been epic poetry, or Homeric poetry, about 1000 B.C., among Asiatic and European Greeks, need not be questioned. But that the Homeric poems, as we now possess them, were reduced to writing much before 7 00 B.C., has never been proved, for the simple reason that the use of alphabetic writing for literary purposes, in the full sense of that w^ord, before that date, has never been established, whether in Greece, or anywhere else.

But whether we place these poems in the form in which we now possess them, 1000 or 700 B.C., it does by no means follow that they represent, as it were, a complete stratum of Greek thought, still less that all we find in later times of Greek thought, language, myth, religion, and philosophy must have passed through that Homeric stratum. This would be takings far too narrow a view of the ancient growth of the Greek intellect. You know with what contempt some of the ancient philosophers of Greece spoke of Homer 314 LECTURE XT.

as one who had degraded the gods by what he sang of them. That shows that they at all events did not consider him as the only, as the earliest and truest representative of the ancient religion of the Greeks. And what applies to religion, applies to everything else. The Homeric poems are a splendid fragment, but they are a fragment only of ancient Greek thought. It is because they are the only fragment left to us of that ancient period, that we have so often been tempted to take them as a complete image of that period, and that we have forgotten that epic poetry in describing the wars and adventures of an heroic race, reflects chiefly the thoughts of one section of ancient society, and does not necessarily reflect the thoughts, the feelings, the customs, and superstitions of the people at large.

The Homeric poems, as you know, tell us very little about the state of the departed, except in that rhapsody of the Odyssey which is called the Nekyia, the journey of Odysseus to the realm of Hades. Many scholars in describing to us what the ancient Greeks thought about life after death, have taken that Nekyia for their chief, nay for their only guide. But this very rhapsody has by some excellent critics been considered as very pecuhar and exceptional, and as being possibly the work of a different, probably a Boeotian poet.

These are points that admit of discussion, but there is nothing to lead us to admit that what Homer tells us about the state of the departed in Hades was all that the Greeks believed about a future life.

Take so simple a question as the disposal of dead bodies. If we took our information from Homer only, we should say that the ancient Greeks burnt SOUL AFTER DEATH. 315 the corpse. But there is a very well supported tradition that in the days of Cecrops the Athenians buried their dead ^.

I believe we may safely say that sacrifices in honour of the dead, the pouring out of blood on the grass, and the burning of victims dedicated to the departed, are all unknown in the Homeric poems. Nay, the same applies to Hesiod also. Whenever a general remark of this kind is made, it always provokes opposition, and great efforts are made to discover some traces in support of the opposite opinion. Thus in our case, the games celebrated by Achilles in honour of Patroklos, and again the pouring out of blood by Odysseus for the shades in Hades, have been pointed out to show that Homer knew of sacrifices for the dead, and of worship of ancestral spirits. But it is too easily forgotten that when such beliefs and customs are once recognised, they do not manifest themselves in a few isolated cases only, but pervade the whole atmosphere of a poem. The funeral honours paid to Patroklos stand by themselves. They are clearly an exceptional event. And the blood poured out by Odysseus to restore the shades of some departed heroes and heroines to life for a short time only, is something totally different from what is meant by ancestral worship ^.

But, in spite of this, I feel by no means inclined to ' Kirchmanni, Be Funerilms Romanorimi, Libri quatuor, 1672, p. 2 ; Cicero, de Legibus, 2, ' Nam et Athenis iam illo more a Cecrope, ut aiunt, permanuit hoc ius teri'a condendi.'

^ What Eohde brings forward in his PstjcJie as proving the existence of ancestral worship in the Homeric poems, had been more fully treated before by P. Stengel in Fleckeisen's Jahrbucher fur Klass. Philologie, 1883, p. 373, ' Einfiihning der in Homerischer Zeit noch nicht bekannten Opfer in Griechenland.'

say that these natural manifestations of human piety were altogether absent in ancient Greece, and took their origin in post-Homeric times only. We are told in fact on very good authority that one of the laws collected by Drakon was ' to honour the gods and the local heroes ^ '. Drakon collected his laws in the seventh century b. c, and even in his time this was called a perpetual law.

I doubt even w4iether a rhapsody like the Nekyia, treating of so lugubrious a subject, could ever have become very popular either at private or public festivals. It was preserved, however, probably for the same reason for which the catalogue of the ships was preserved. It became something like the golden book of the Venetian republic. To have the names of certain heroes or heroines entered in the Nekyia, became like a title of nobility to the families or localities to w^hich the most illustrious of the departed heroes and heroines belonged, and there can be little doubt that this very natural ambition led to the sub' sequent additions to the illustrious roll of the names both of famous ancestors and of renowned cities.

If we keep all this in mind, we shall more easily understand two things, — first, that the state of the departed should be so rarely and only so vaguely alluded to in the Homeric poems ; and secondly, that the Nekyia should in a less degree than other portions of the Iliad and Odyssey, reflect the universal thoughts of the Greeks on the mysterious subject of death and life after death.

' Cf. Porphyrins, De Abstinentia, iv. '22, 'Erret Kal ApaKovros vofxo^ HvrjfxoviiXTai toiovtos, Oerrfxus aiwvios tois 'ArOiba Viixoixivois, Kvptos tuv airavTa xp^voVs 9(ov$ Ti/J-dv Kal T]pcxjas ^'^xocpiovs.

SOUL AFTER DEATH. 317 We knoAv that this subject, the state of the dead, is at all times and in all countries one of the vaguest, one almost entirely determined by individual hopes and fears and imaginations, one on which the human heart is ready to believe almost anything, and one on which human wisdom can say nothing. In the Homeric Nekyia we may see a picture of the lower world — if it is indeed a lower world — adapted to the great epic drama of which it forms a part ; but I doubt whether we can accept it as a true representation of the ideas entertained by the fathers and mothers or children in the small homes of ancient Greece, as to the state of those for whom theii* hearts were bleeding and yearning.
Homer does not reflect popular Opinion on Death.

Let us consider a few points only, on which it seems to me quite clear that Homer does not reflect the general feeling of the Greeks with regard to the dead. One of the commonest names in Greek given to the dead is jiaKapes, the blest. It is found in Hesiod, who knows of the islands of the blest, iiaKapcav vtjo-ol, and in nearly all subsequent writers. Homer, however, never speaks of the dead as blest, but represents them as utterly miserable. You remember, how even Achilles, though honoured like a king among the departed, would rather be ' a serf on the land of a poor and portionless man than rule over all the dead who have come to nought' (Od. xi. 488).

If the Greeks at large had really shared Homer's idea that even the best of men were in the next world eidola or shades, without energy, without real self-consciousness, incapable of action and enjoyment, though 318 LECTURE XI.
retaining their names and, so far. their identit}^ [a\xevy]va Kdpi]va), they could never have called them the blest.

It might seem indeed from a passage in the Odyssey, beautifully, but yet not quite accurately, rendered by Mr. Gladstone, as if Homer had sometimes admitted in his heroes and heroines in Hades a continuance of their human feelings of love. He translates the words of Penelope (Od. xx. 79) :
' Would fair-haired Artemis had stayed my breath, So might I pass below the hated earth, Yearn for Odysseus, even there in death, Nor live to cheer a soul of meaner worth.'

But the true meaning is, not that she might yearn for Odysseus there, below the hated earth, but that looking, 3^eaniing for Odysseus, she might pass below the hated earth. This may seem a small difference, but it involves important issues.

In spite of the popularity of the Homeric poems, I doubt whether the Greeks really believed that their favourite hero, Achilles, was such as Homer describes him, mourning his dreary fate in the realm of Hades. Their thoughts are more truly reflected, I believe, in the popular verses on Harmodius and Aristogiton, where we read :
' Dearest Harmodius, thou art surely not dead. Thou dwellest, they say, in the isles of the blest. Where the swift-footed Achilles, Where the son of Tydeus, the brave Diomedes, dwells.'

Here we see a very diflferent Achilles from that of Homer, dwelling in the isles of the blest with Diomedes, the bravest of the brave, and with Harmodius and Aristogiton. who delivered Greece from her tyrants.

Small as Greece seems to us, it was always full of SOUL AFTEK DEATH. 319 life, full of individuality, full of variety. As a rule, we may say, no doubt, that all the Greeks looked at death as the greatest of misfortunes. Yet no one was so ready as the Greek to give up his life for his country, and to prefer death with honour to life with dishonour. Some of the Greeks also had soon discovered the sorrow that is inseparable from life. With all their enjoyment of life, even the Greeks called mortal men 5eiAot, wretched. Homer also calls them frequently becXol (SpoToC, miserable mortals ! And when we go a little further we find a poet like Theognis in the sixth century proclaiming his conviction that it is ' best for man not to be born and to see the rays of the burning sun, and, if born, then as soon as possible to pass through the gates of Hades, and to lie down, having heaped up much earth as his grave.'

zh: 你還記得我在第一期講座中向你們提及的芬蘭人吧。他們既不屬於雅利安種族，也不屬於閃語族種族。他們說烏拉‧阿爾泰語族中的一種語言。他們現在部分住在瑞典，部分住在俄羅斯。你們也許還記得葛拉德斯頓先生^最近在蘇格蘭時，為他們聲援，將他們視為世界上受壓迫的民族之一。他們是否適合自治統治，以及葛拉德斯頓先生的倡議是否有可能為他們獲得自治統治的利益，同時在聖彼得堡的帝國議會中獲得席位——當存在這樣的議會之時——這些是不關乎我們的事項。但與我們有關的是，在芬蘭農民之中，在不識讀寫的人民之中，在本世紀上半葉發現了大量的史詩片段，這些片段完全是靠口頭傳統保存下來的，從未被詩人或其欣賞者筆錄成文，然而卻輕易地能夠組合成一部完整的史詩。我希望我有時間向你們解釋這些詩歌被保存下來，最後被蒐集、印刷、批判性編訂和翻譯的過程^。但我想 ^ 見《泰晤士報》，1890年10月29日。 ^^ 見附錄VIII。
靈魂死後。313 從這些簡短的評論中，你將已看出，在什麼意義上通俗詩歌（例如荷馬詩篇）可以說既反映了一個詩人心靈的思想，同時也反映了眾多民眾的思想——這些民眾本來不會傾聽，也就是說，他們不會容許任何詩歌存續，除非是他們自己贊同的作品。
《亡靈篇》(Nekyia)並不代表民間信仰。
現在產生了一個問題。我們是否可以將荷馬關於死者所言及暗示的內容——特別是他的《冥界之旅》（Nekyia）——視為反映了他那個時代希臘人的普遍觀念？我對此表示懷疑。
首先，人們所謂的「他的時代」究竟指的是什麼？誠然，大約在西元前一千年，亞洲及歐洲的希臘人之間可能曾經存在過史詩詩歌或荷馬式詩歌。但我們現在所擁有的《荷馬史詩》在西元前七百年之前就已成文，這一點從未被證明過，原因很簡單，因為在那個時期之前，無論在希臘或其他任何地方，都從未確立過字母文字在完整意義上用於文學目的的使用。
但是，無論我們將這些詩歌置於我們現在所擁有的形式中，定年在西元前一千年或七百年，這絕不意味著它們代表了希臘思想的某個完整層次，更遠不足以說明我們在後來希臘思想、語言、神話、宗教和哲學中所發現的一切都必然經歷過荷馬式的層次。這將是對古代希臘智識成長的過於狹隘的看法。你知道古代希臘的某些哲學家曾以何等輕蔑的態度談論荷馬。

作為一個貶低了眾神的人，因為他所唱誦的內容而貶低了他們。這表明他們至少並不認為他是古代希臘宗教唯一、最早、最真實的代表。而凡是適用於宗教的原理，也適用於其他一切事物。荷馬史詩是一個光輝燦爛的片段，但它終究只是片段而已，是古代希臘思想的片段。正因為它是我們僅存的那個古代時期的唯一片段，我們才一再被誘使把它當作那個時期的完整圖景，而忘記了史詩在描述英雄種族的戰爭與冒險時，主要反映的是古代社會某一階層的思想，並不必然反映人民大眾的思想、感受、風俗和迷信。
《荷馬史詩》如你所知，除了《奧德賽》中那段稱為《降冥記》（Nekyia）的詩篇——敘述奧德修斯前往冥王哈得斯領域的旅程——之外，對於亡者的狀態幾乎沒有甚麼記載。許多學者在向我們描述古代希臘人對於死後生命的看法時，便以該《降冥記》為其主要的、甚至說是唯一的指南。然而，這同一篇詩段卻被一些傑出的批評家認為極其特殊且例外，並且很可能出自另一位詩人之手，或許是某位波奧蒂亞詩人的作品。
這些都是可以討論的要點，但沒有任何證據足以讓我們承認，荷馬告訴我們關於亡者在哈迪斯中的狀態，就是希臘人對於來世所信仰的全部內容。
以死體處置這樣一個簡單的問題為例。若我們僅從荷馬獲得信息，應會說古代希臘人火化死者的屍體。315 但有一個傳統証據充分支持：在刻克羅普斯時代，雅典人埋葬他們的死者。^

我認為我們可以安全地說，向亡者致祭、在草地上澆灑血液，以及焚燒獻給逝者的祭牲，在荷馬史詩中都是聞所未聞的。不僅如此，赫西俄德的作品中也同樣沒有這些現象。每當做出這類籠統的陳述時，總會引發反對意見，人們會費力地發掘証據來支持相反的觀點。因此在我們的例子中，有人指出阿基里斯為紀念帕特羅克洛斯所舉行的競技會，以及奧德修斯在冥府中為亡靈澆灑的血液，以此證明荷馬知曉對亡者的祭祀與祖靈崇拜。然而人們太容易忽略的是，當這類信仰與習俗一旦被真正確認，它們絕不會只在少數孤立的事例中顯現，而是會瀰漫於整部詩作的氛圍之中。對帕特羅克洛斯的喪葬敬禮是獨立存在的，顯然是一樁特殊事件。而奧德修斯澆灑的血液，其目的只是短暫地讓某些已故英雄與女英雄的亡靈復活，這與人們所說的祖靈崇拜在本質上完全不同 ^。
但儘管如此，我絕不傾向於……¹ ¹ Kirchmanni, *De Funerilms Romanorimi*, 卷四，1672，第2頁；西塞羅，《法律篇》第2卷，「蓋在雅典，據稱自刻克羅普斯以來，即保持此埋葬死者之法律」(*Nam et Athenis iam illo more a Cecrope, ut aiunt, permanuit hoc ius teri'a condendi*)。

埃歐德（Eohde）在其《心理學》（Psyche）中提出的關於《荷馬史詩》中祖先祭祀存在的證據，早已由彼得‧斯滕格爾（P. Stengel）在《弗萊凱森古典語言學年刊》（Fleckeisen's Jahrbücher für Klass. Philologie）1883年第373頁中以更詳盡的方式處理過了，其題為《荷馬時代尚未為人所知的祭祀在希臘的引入》（'Einführung der in Homerischer Zeit noch nicht bekannten Opfer in Griechenland'）。
說這些人類虔誠的自然表現在古代希臘完全缺失，只在後荷馬時代才出現，這是不對的。實際上，我們從極為可靠的權威來源得知，德拉古所編纂的法律中有一條是「尊敬諸神與本地英雄 ^」。德拉古在公元前七世紀編纂他的法律，即使在他那個時代，這也被稱為永恆之法。

我甚至懷疑《冥界篇》（Nekyia）這樣一部涉及如此陰鬱主題的敍事詩，是否曾在私人或公眾慶典上獲得很大的歡迎。然而它之所以被保存下來，可能是出於與《船隊目錄》被保存相同的原因。它變成了類似於威尼斯共和國的黃金冊。讓某些英雄或女英雄的名字被列入《冥界篇》之中，對於那些最傑出的亡故英雄和女英雄所屬的家族或地域而言，便成為了一種貴族身分的標誌。毫無疑問，這種極為自然的野心促使人們後來不斷在這份顯赫的名冊中增添著名祖先和聞名城邦的名字。
如果我們牢記這一切，便能更容易理解兩件事：其一，亡者之狀態在《荷馬史詩》中竟然甚少被提及，且只被含糊其辭地暗示；其二，《冥府之旅》（Nekyia）相比《伊利亞德》和《奧德賽》的其他篇章，應該更少反映希臘人對死亡與來世這一神秘主題的普遍思想。

參見波斐利《論禁慾》第四卷第二十二節，「曾有一位法律制定者被這樣銘記：永恆的律法為雅典人所遵守，主宰一切時代者規定尊崇眾神與英雄。」(Erret Kal ApaKovros vofxo^ HvrjfxoviiXTai toiovtos, Oerrfxus aiwvios tois 'ArOiba Viixoixivois, Kvptos tuv airavTa xp^voVs 9(ov$ Ti/J-dv Kal T]pcxjas ^'^xocpiovs.)

我們知道，這個主題——亡者的狀態——在所有時代和所有國家中，都是最模糊不清的主題之一，幾乎完全由個人的希望、恐懼和想像所決定，是人心準備相信幾乎任何事物的主題，也是人類智慧無法言說任何確定之事的主題。在荷馬的《冥府之旅》(Homeric Nekyia)中，我們可以看到冥府景象的圖像——如果它確實是冥府的話——其設計適配於作為其組成部分的宏偉史詩戲劇；然而，我懷疑我們是否能將其接受為古代希臘小家庭中的父親、母親或孩子所抱持的觀念的真實再現，即關於那些使他們心靈流血和渴望的逝者的狀態。
荷馬並未反映民眾對死亡的普遍看法。

讓我們只考慮幾個要點，在這些方面似乎很清楚荷馬並未反映希臘人對死者的普遍看法。希臘人給予死者最常見的名字之一是 μακαρες（馬卡雷斯），即「有福的人」。這個詞見於赫西俄德，他知曉有福者之島 μακαρων νησοι（馬卡隆 尼索伊），也見於幾乎所有後來的作家。然而，荷馬卻從不稱死者為有福的人，反而將他們描繪為完全悲慘的。你還記得，即使阿基琉斯在冥府中受到如國王般的尊敬，他寧願「在某個貧窮無地產之人的土地上做苦役，也不願統治所有已化為烏有的死者」(Od. xi. 488)。
如果希臘人普遍真正認同荷馬的觀點，即使是最優秀的人在來世也不過是幽靈（eidola）或陰影，沒有活力、沒有真實的自我意識、無法行動也無法享樂，那麼318 講座XI。
保留其名號，並在某種程度上保持其同一性（梵：*avi* *kāpi* *kāpi*），他們決不可能稱呼他們為「有福者」。
從《奧德賽》中的一個段落看來，確實似乎荷馬有時允許他筆下英雄與英雌在冥府中保持人類的愛的情感。格拉德斯通先生雖有優美的譯筆，卻並非完全準確。他如此譯述珀涅羅珀的言辭（《奧德賽》第二十卷第七十九行）：
願金髮的阿爾忒彌斯奪去我的呼吸， 如此我便能通過令人厭惡的地下， 縱使死後仍渴慕奧德修斯， 也不願活著去取悅一個更卑微的靈魂。
但其真正的含義並非她可能在令人厭惡的土地下方渴望奧德修斯，而是說她在尋求、渴望奧德修斯的同時，得以穿過令人厭惡的土地而下行。這看似微小的區別，卻涉及重要的問題。
儘管荷馬史詩廣受歡迎，我懷疑希臘人是否真的相信他們心愛的英雄阿基里斯（Achilles）真如荷馬所述，在哈迪斯（Hades）的陰冥之境中為其淒涼的命運而哀悼。我相信，他們的思想更真實地反映在關於哈莫迪烏斯和阿里斯托季同（Harmodius and Aristogiton）的民間詩歌中，其中我們讀到：
「最親愛的哈爾莫狄烏斯啊，你確實並未死去。他們說，你住在福樂之島。那裡是迅足的阿基里斯所在之處，那裡也是提迪斯之子、勇敢的狄俄墨得斯所住之地。」
在此我們看到了與荷馬筆下迥然不同的阿基里斯形象，他與最勇敢的勇士狄奧墨得斯，以及解救希臘脫離暴君統治的哈莫迪烏斯與亞里斯托吉同，共同居住在有福者的島嶼上。

希臘雖然對我們來說顯得渺小，卻總是充滿了生命、充滿了個性、充滿了多樣性。一般而言，我們無疑可以說，所有的希臘人都把死亡視為最大的不幸。然而沒有人比希臘人更願意為國家獻出生命，更願意選擇有榮譽的死亡而非恥辱的生活。有些希臘人也很早就發現了那與生命密不可分的悲痛。儘管希臘人盡享生命之樂，他們甚至稱凡人為 δειλοί（deiloi），即可憐的人。荷馬也經常稱他們為 δειλοί θροτοί（deiloi throtoi），即可悲的凡人！當我們進一步探索時，會發現像第六世紀的詩人西奧格尼斯這樣的聲音，宣示他的信念：「人最好不要被生下來，不要看到炎熱太陽的光芒；若已出生，則應盡快通過冥府的大門，安息於下，讓許多泥土堆成自己的墳墓。」
我注意到您提供的文本似乎是亂碼或損壞的希臘文字符，而非可讀的英文原文。文本「ndvTOJV fikv fiTj <pvvai enixOovioiaiv apiarov M778' kaiSeiv avyas o^eos rjfXiov...」無法進行有意義的翻譯。 請提供清晰的英文原文，我將按照您的規則進行準確的繁體中文翻譯。
